one_line_takeaway: RPP is a black-box, certified, sample-level backdoor-poisoning detector that uses conformal-calibrated stability of a model's predicted probabilities under Gaussian noise perturbation, and it is specifically designed to remain effective as backdoor defenses like AC, ASSET, and SCALE-UP degrade sharply (TPR falling toward 0% in the paper's own MNIST experiments) under class imbalance.
key_finding: From the paper's own tables (Table 1, MNIST vs BadNets, mu=0.9): as imbalance ratio rho increases from 2 to 200, baseline defense TPR collapses -- e.g. AC drops from TPR 61.8% (rho=2) to 0.1% (rho=200); ASSET drops from TPR 88.1% (rho=2) to 0.0% (rho=200); SCALE-UP drops from TPR 98.9% (rho=2) to 0.1% (rho=200). From Table 2 (CIFAR-10, mu=0.9): at rho=2, RPP ('Ours') achieves TPR 96.7%/FPR 3.2% against BadNets, comparable-or-better TPR than most of the 12 baselines at generally lower or comparable FPR across the 10 attack types listed (Badnets, Blend, Trojan, SIG, ISSBA, WaNet, Sleeper Agent, AdaPatch, AdaBlend, Narcissus); at rho=100, RPP attains TPR 100.0%/FPR 9.2% against BadNets while most baselines (SS, AC, RE, ABL, CT, ASSET) collapse toward TPR near 0%, and RPP maintains meaningfully higher TPR than these baselines across attack types while SCALE-UP and MSPC sometimes exceed RPP's TPR but at markedly higher FPR (paper flags these instances in red in its own table). Certification results (Sec. 6.2, alpha=0.05): SVHN certification coverage 98.8%-100.0% (FPR 1.2%-23.9%), CIFAR-10 90.4%-99.9% (FPR 5.9%-29.6%), TinyImageNet 99.3%-100.0% (FPR 2.0%-31.2%), ImageNet10 84.5%-96.7% (FPR 11.6%-24.6%), across balanced and imbalanced (rho=2,10,100,200) settings.
core_contribution: The paper makes two contributions. (1) An empirical threat investigation showing that dataset class imbalance amplifies backdoor-attack success and degrades existing prior-training backdoor defenses (e.g., AC, ASSET, SCALE-UP), because these defenses rely on distribution-level signals (classwise statistics, clustering, global thresholds) that become biased and unstable when classes are imbalanced. (2) RPP (Randomized Probability Perturbation), a new black-box, sample-level, certified poisoned-sample detector: for a given input, RPP measures the expected L-infinity change in the model's predicted probability vector under injected isotropic Gaussian noise (empirical RPP, eRPP, averaged over J noisy copies). Because poisoned samples' predictions are stable under noise (the trigger dominates the prediction) while clean samples' predictions fluctuate more, low eRPP indicates poisoning. A conformal-prediction procedure (using a small clean calibration set) calibrates a distribution-free detection threshold q-hat, and the paper derives (Thm. 5.1/Cor. 5.2) a certified condition on trigger norm ||delta||_2 relative to noise scale sigma under which poisoned samples are guaranteed detectable, plus (Thm. 5.3/5.4) a conformal calibration guarantee and an upper bound on the false positive rate (FPR <= alpha + 1/(n+1), asymptotically approaching alpha).
threat_model: Data-poisoning backdoor attacks (not model-manipulation backdoors) in K-class classification: the adversary can inject/mislabel a portion of training data (stamp trigger delta, relabel to attacker-chosen target class y_t) but cannot control the training procedure or final model. The paper explicitly excludes the model-manipulation backdoor threat model (attacker controlling training) and excludes multi-trigger/multi-target backdoors. A specific imbalance-aware variant is studied: the attacker is assumed aware of the victim dataset's class distribution and strategically poisons minority-class samples, relabeling them to a majority class, which the paper shows increases attack success rate and reduces the required poisoning budget. The defender operates as a prior-training (before-training) detector with black-box access only to a preliminarily trained model's output probabilities plus a small clean calibration set drawn from the same distribution as the training data.
limitations: The paper's own stated/derivable limitations: (1) The certification requires ||delta||_2 >= a threshold R that depends on sigma, p(x), and the calibration-derived bound zeta(x,delta); very small-norm triggers fall outside the certified regime, though the authors argue (not fully proven) such triggers need impractically high poisoning budgets to achieve high ASR. (2) Certification depends on Assumption A.1 (noise does not increase the poisoned sample's target-class probability), described by the authors as 'reasonable and common' but not universally proven; only empirical support is offered (App. C.4). (3) In several attack/defense combinations in Table 2, SCALE-UP and MSPC achieve higher TPR than RPP, so RPP is not uniformly dominant -- it wins on the TPR/FPR trade-off rather than on TPR alone. (4) The method excludes model-manipulation backdoors and multi-trigger/multi-target backdoor attacks by explicit threat-model scoping, not evaluation. (5) Requires a brief preliminary training pass on the (possibly poisoned) dataset before detection, and a clean, i.i.d. calibration set from the same distribution -- an assumption about calibration-data availability and purity that could be a practical constraint. (6) FPR bound (Thm. 5.4) is asymptotic (holds exactly only as n -> infinity) with a finite-sample slack of 1/(n+1).

quantitative_results: Table 1 (MNIST, BadNets, mu=0.9): AC TPR 61.8% (rho=2) -> 0.1% (rho=200); ASSET TPR 88.1% (rho=2) -> 0.0% (rho=200); SCALE-UP TPR 98.9% (rho=2) -> 0.1% (rho=200)
Table 2 (CIFAR-10, BadNets, rho=2, mu=0.9): RPP (Ours) TPR 96.7% / FPR 3.2%
Table 2 (CIFAR-10, BadNets, rho=100, mu=0.9): RPP (Ours) TPR 100.0% / FPR 9.2%

Certification at alpha=0.05: SVHN coverage 98.8-100.0% (FPR 1.2-23.9%); CIFAR-10 coverage 90.4-99.9% (FPR 5.9-29.6%); TinyImageNet coverage 99.3-100.0% (FPR 2.0-31.2%); ImageNet10 coverage 84.5-96.7% (FPR 11.6-24.6%)
Threshold q-hat for RPP distributions separating clean/poisoned: ~0.001 at rho=200 (imbalanced) vs. ~0.5 for balanced datasets
